key_insight: Plastic deformation is modeled as coming from lattice-free oblate spheroidal transformation zones (OSTZs), with dislocations emerging only as collective excitations of many OSTZs.
engineering_relevance: This gives a unified constitutive framework for predicting plasticity across crystals, grain boundaries, ceramics, glasses, and nanocrystals where classical dislocation theory is incomplete.
method: Uses Eshelby inclusion theory to define a shear-eigenstrained oblate spheroid as the elementary plastic carrier.
Proves that a single OSTZ gives a finite elastic dipole, while a co-planar chain of OSTZs becomes mathematically equivalent to a Peierls–Nabarro dislocation.
Derives strength- and defect-related quantities such as Peierls stress, core energy, Frank–Read critical stress, and stacking-fault energy from OSTZ geometry.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports validation against grain-boundary-sliding data, literature across metals/ceramics/bulk metallic glasses, and a 41-system compilation, with fitted dilatational and shear eigenstrains reproduced to within 2% and 15% respectively.
watch_item: Because the framework is highly idealized and geometry-driven, practical use may depend on how well OSTZ shape and eigenstrain parameters can be identified for a real microstructure.
paper_title: Oblate Spheroid Excitation Theory: A Unified, Lattice-Free Foundation for Plastic Deformation from Which Dislocations Emerge as Collective Excitations
